questions.q: Which datum determines a rank-$n$ vector bundle $E \to X$ up to isomorphism, given an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$?
choices: A choice of basis for each fibre $E_x$
Transition functions $g_{\alpha\beta}\colon U_\alpha \cap U_\beta \to GL_n$ satisfying the cocycle condition $g_{\alpha\beta} g_{\beta\gamma} = g_{\alpha\gamma}$
questions.hint: Local triviality plus how the trivialisations agree on overlaps.
questions.explain: A bundle is glued from local trivialisations $E|_{U_\alpha} \cong U_\alpha\times\mathbb{R}^n$, and the transition data $g_{\alpha\beta}$ — subject to the cocycle condition — determines the bundle. Global sections need not exist (a global trivialisation is too strong).

questions.hint: Which one has no nowhere-vanishing section that returns to the same value after one loop?
questions.explain: The Möbius bundle is the rank-$1$ bundle on $S^1$ whose monodromy multiplies by $-1$; it admits no nowhere-vanishing section, hence is non-trivial. $T\mathbb{R}^n$ is trivial, and $TS^3$ is trivial because $S^3$ is parallelisable (it is a Lie group).

questions.q: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $f\colon N \to M$ a smooth map. The pullback $f^*TM$ is naturally identified with which bundle on $N$?
choices: The bundle whose fibre over $p\in N$ is $T_{f(p)}M$
questions.hint: Pullback rebuilds the fibres above $f(p)$ over $p$.
questions.explain: By definition $(f^*TM)_p = T_{f(p)}M$. When $f$ is an immersion, $f^*TM \cong TN \oplus \nu_f$, but in general $f^*TM \ne TN$.

questions.q: What does the vanishing of $w_1(E)$ measure for a real vector bundle $E \to X$?
choices: Existence of a spin structure on $E$
questions.hint: It is the mod-$2$ obstruction to a coherent choice of frame orientation.
questions.explain: $w_1(E) \in H^1(X;\mathbb{Z}/2)$ classifies the determinant line bundle $\det E$ up to isomorphism; it vanishes iff $E$ is orientable. Triviality is much stronger; a spin structure requires $w_2 = 0$ on top of $w_1 = 0$.

questions.q: By the Whitney sum formula, $w(E \oplus F) = w(E) \smile w(F)$. What is $w_2(E\oplus F)$ in terms of the $w_i$?
questions.hint: Expand $(1 + w_1(E) + w_2(E) + \cdots)(1 + w_1(F) + w_2(F) + \cdots)$ and read off the degree-$2$ piece.
questions.explain: Multiplying total classes and collecting degree-$2$ terms gives $w_2(E\oplus F) = w_2(E) + w_1(E) w_1(F) + w_2(F)$. The cross term $w_1 \smile w_1$ is exactly what $w_2$ feels under direct sum.

questions.q: For the tangent bundle of $\mathbb{RP}^2$, which Stiefel–Whitney class is non-zero?
questions.explain: $T\mathbb{RP}^2 \oplus \mathbb{R} \cong (\gamma^*)^{\oplus 3}$ gives $w(T\mathbb{RP}^2) = (1+a)^3 = 1 + 3a + 3a^2 = 1 + a + a^2$ in $H^*(\mathbb{RP}^2;\mathbb{Z}/2) = \mathbb{Z}/2[a]/(a^3)$, so $w_1 = a \ne 0$ (non-orientable) and $w_2 = a^2 \ne 0$ (no spin structure).

questions.q: In which cohomology group does the Chern class $c_i(E)$ of a complex rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ live?
questions.hint: Complex bundles double the cohomological degree compared with real ones.
questions.explain: Each Chern class lives in even integral degree: $c_i(E) \in H^{2i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. The total Chern class $c(E) = 1 + c_1 + \cdots + c_n$ assembles them in $H^{\mathrm{even}}(X;\mathbb{Z})$.

questions.q: On $X = \mathbb{CP}^1 = S^2$, complex line bundles are classified by which integer invariant?
questions.hint: $H^2(\mathbb{CP}^1;\mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ and $c_1$ is the universal characteristic class.
questions.explain: Pic$(\mathbb{CP}^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, with the isomorphism $L \mapsto \deg L = \langle c_1(L), [\mathbb{CP}^1]\rangle$. The line bundle $\mathcal{O}(d)$ has $c_1 = d \cdot h$ where $h$ is the generator of $H^2$.

questions.q: If $E \to X$ is a complex rank-$n$ bundle, the top Chern class $c_n(E)$ agrees with which other class of the underlying real bundle?
choices: The Euler class $e(E_\mathbb{R})$ of the underlying oriented real $2n$-bundle
questions.hint: Both classes obstruct the existence of a nowhere-vanishing section.
questions.explain: A complex structure on a real bundle gives a canonical orientation, and the top Chern class $c_n(E) \in H^{2n}(X;\mathbb{Z})$ equals the Euler class $e(E_\mathbb{R}) \in H^{2n}(X;\mathbb{Z})$ — both count zeros of a generic section.

questions.q: By definition, $p_i(E) = ?$ for a real bundle $E$.
questions.hint: Take the complexification, keep only even Chern classes, and absorb a sign.
questions.explain: $p_i(E) := (-1)^i c_{2i}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) \in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. The sign is conventional; odd Chern classes of $E\otimes\mathbb{C}$ are 2-torsion because $E\otimes\mathbb{C}\cong \overline{E\otimes\mathbb{C}}$ as complex bundles.

questions.q: What cohomological degree does $p_i(E)$ live in?
questions.hint: Complexification doubles degrees, and we keep only even Chern classes.
questions.explain: Since $c_{2i}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) \in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$, Pontryagin classes are concentrated in degrees divisible by $4$.

questions.q: Why are the odd Chern classes $c_{2i+1}(E\otimes\mathbb{C})$ ignored in defining Pontryagin classes?
choices: They are 2-torsion (annihilated by $2$), and the integral lift is captured by the even classes
questions.hint: $E\otimes\mathbb{C}$ is isomorphic to its conjugate as a complex bundle.
questions.explain: The complexification of a real bundle is isomorphic to its complex conjugate, so $c_k(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) = (-1)^k c_k(E\otimes\mathbb{C})$, forcing $2 c_{2i+1}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) = 0$. The honest integral information lives in even degrees.

questions.q: For a closed oriented smooth manifold $M^n$, what does $\langle e(TM), [M] \rangle$ compute?
questions.hint: Poincaré–Hopf: count signed zeros of any vector field.
questions.explain: By Poincaré–Hopf, for any vector field $X$ on $M$ with isolated zeros, $\sum_p \ind_p(X) = \chi(M)$, and the same number is computed by pairing the Euler class with the fundamental class.

questions.hint: $T^2 = S^1 \times S^1$ is parallelisable, so $TT^2$ admits a nowhere-vanishing section.
questions.explain: The torus has a nowhere-vanishing vector field (e.g. $\partial/\partial\theta_1$), so by Poincaré–Hopf $\chi(T^2) = 0$. Equivalently, the Betti numbers $1,2,1$ give $\chi = 1-2+1 = 0$.

questions.q: On which space does an oriented rank-$n$ bundle fail to have a nowhere-vanishing section iff $e(E) \ne 0$?
choices: On any reasonable base — the Euler class is the universal primary obstruction in $H^n$
Only on closed manifolds of dimension $n$
questions.hint: Obstruction theory expresses non-vanishing of a rank-$n$ section as an obstruction in $H^n$.
questions.explain: For an oriented rank-$n$ bundle on a CW base, the primary obstruction to a nowhere-vanishing section is exactly the Euler class $e(E) \in H^n(X;\mathbb{Z})$. Higher obstructions can appear but the first one is always $e(E)$.

questions.q: Given a connection $\nabla$ with curvature $F_\nabla$ on a complex bundle, which polynomial gives the total Chern class?
questions.hint: The factor $\frac{i}{2\pi}$ is exactly the normalisation that produces integral classes.
questions.explain: Chern–Weil: $c(E) = \det\!\big(I + \frac{i}{2\pi}F_\nabla\big)$ as a closed $\mathrm{Ad}$-invariant form on $M$, whose de Rham class is independent of $\nabla$. Expanding in degrees gives $c_k$.

questions.q: On a closed oriented surface $\Sigma$ with Riemannian metric, the Gauss–Bonnet theorem $\int_\Sigma K\,dA = 2\pi\,\chi(\Sigma)$ is which Chern–Weil identity?
choices: $e(T\Sigma)$ paired against $[\Sigma]$, i.e. the rank-$1$ Chern–Weil formula
questions.hint: $\Sigma$ is $2$-dimensional, so we need a degree-$2$ class — and we are computing $\chi$.
questions.explain: $T\Sigma$ is an oriented rank-$2$ real bundle, equivalently a complex line bundle; the curvature $2$-form is $-K\,dA$ (up to sign), and $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_\Sigma K\,dA = \chi(\Sigma)$ is the Chern–Weil expression of the Euler/first-Chern number.

questions.q: Why is the de Rham class of $P(F_\nabla)$ independent of the connection $\nabla$?
choices: Because $F_{\nabla} = F_{\nabla'}$ for any two connections
Because $P(F_{\nabla'}) - P(F_\nabla) = d\,(\text{transgression form})$ is exact
questions.hint: Two connections differ by an $\mathrm{End}(E)$-valued $1$-form; build a path between them.
questions.explain:
If $
abla_t = 
abla + t(
abla' - 
abla)$ is a path of connections, integrating $ rac{d}{dt}P(F_{
abla_t})$ over $t \in [0,1]$ produces an explicit primitive, the Chern–Simons / transgression form, so $[P(F_
abla)]$ is independent of $
abla$ in de Rham cohomology.
